coredata.dc:title: Thermal management systems for batteries in electric vehicles: A recent review
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Hafiz Muhammad
coredata.dc:description: IC engines in transportation sector are seen as a major cause of increasing air pollution. Electric Vehicles (EVs) are deemed as a green energy solution for pollution free future. The energy source of a modern-day EV is a Lithium ion battery pack. Temperature sensitivity is a major limitation for the lithium-ion battery performance and so the prevalent battery thermal management systems (BTMS) are reviewed in this study for practical implications. Firstly, the design considerations are analyzed to measure value of thermal safety and the international market potential is studied in this manuscript. Secondly, thermal stability analysis is drawn for thermal runaway situations, extreme weather conditions and aging phenomenon. The effect of sub-zero temperature, charging/discharging rates, failures caused by short circuiting, lack of temperature uniformity and mechanical deformation are also evaluated. In addition, the theoretical and experimental models of various battery thermal management strategies studied by different authors are compared for their effectiveness in hybrid and electric vehicles. The pinnacle of current review is to assess the upcoming challenges and substantial gaps in the approach of thermal management predicted by researchers and the latest practical methods used by EV manufacturers. Lastly, the optimization of BTMS is considered highly critical for the dominance of electric vehicles, by enhancement in lifespan and overall safety.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: IC engines in transportation sector are seen as a major cause of increasing air pollution. Electric Vehicles (EVs) are deemed as a green energy solution for pollution free future. The energy source of a modern-day EV is a Lithium ion battery pack. Temperature sensitivity is a major limitation for the lithium-ion battery performance and so the prevalent battery thermal management systems (BTMS) are reviewed in this study for practical implications. Firstly, the design considerations are analyzed to measure value of thermal safety and the international market potential is studied in this manuscript. Secondly, thermal stability analysis is drawn for thermal runaway situations, extreme weather conditions and aging phenomenon. The effect of sub-zero temperature, charging/discharging rates, failures caused by short circuiting, lack of temperature uniformity and mechanical deformation are also evaluated. In addition, the theoretical and experimental models of various battery thermal management strategies studied by different authors are compared for their effectiveness in hybrid and electric vehicles. The pinnacle of current review is to assess the upcoming challenges and substantial gaps in the approach of thermal management predicted by researchers and the latest practical methods used by EV manufacturers. Lastly, the optimization of BTMS is considered highly critical for the dominance of electric vehicles, by enhancement in lifespan and overall safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: There have been increasing concerns in general population regarding the world’s current energy and environmental policies facing the world’s automotive industry. Considering the grim energy and environmental circumstances, a much-needed tactical alteration in field of conventional transportation and energy has led the upsurge of vehicle development based upon renewable and not detrimental technology. Undesirable emissions caused by IC engines have negatively impacted, escalating human health and welfare issues by deterioration of air quality leading to global warming (Agarwal and Mustafi, 2021; Bagheri et al., 2021; Rossner et al., 2021; Abdel-Rahman, 1998). The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) which is concerned with control of harmful emissions like carbon monoxide, lead, nitrogen dioxide, ozone, and others as the pollutants of primary concern, called the Criteria Pollutants. EPA reports that the on-road vehicles today are accountable for approximately 85% of petroleum in U.S. is burnt in engines of transportation sector, producing one-third of the U.S. greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Jung et al., 2018).

This motivates scientists and engineers to use different advanced technology to put an end to the exhaust gas emissions from IC engines. Keeping these concerns in mind, the only viable option is to adopt the shift of transportation sector to zero emission vehicles like the electric vehicles. Meyer et al. (2016) stated that “An EV is a Mission-intensive car. It should not be expected to be all things to all people.” This statement might have been true in 20th century but today every form of vehicle from a sports car to an airplane has an electric version of itself. The first known EVs which used non-rechargeable batteries were introduced in 1830s and were commercially available by the end of 19th century (Høyer, 2008). EVs have outlasted many other technologies that have become obsolete; nevertheless they have not been as successful as IC engine vehicles which have high ranges. However, the above-mentioned environmental concerns and advancements in battery technology have paved way for development of electric vehicles.

In all electric and most hybrid vehicles, battery is the key source of energy and power, which has zero emissions. As a matter of fact, the working efficiency of IC engines is one-third as compared to that of an EV, but the latter has an extremely low fuel density (Gustafsson and Johansson, 2015; Fyhr et al., 2017; Fang et al., 2020).

However, the lithium ion battery which is substantially lighter than other electrochemical based batteries has been a highly attractive option as power source of future EVs. Establishing the credibility of batteries based on lithium ion, it is important to discover its shortcomings, which hinders its mass customization. Weighing the pros and cons of different lithium based electrochemical batteries; it is safe to state that lithium-ion battery is a better option as it has high energy density, is not expensive and has no major problems except its safety and performance at elevated temperatures. The thermal management requirement of lithium-ion batteries is not just an assumption, but it has been studied and practically proven by many researchers. Drake et al. (2015) theoretically and practically explored the influence of temperature on charging/discharging rates of lithium ion battery and found that the significant heat generation hindered the battery performance. Moreover, Zhang et al. (2017) worked on the Lithium-ion battery fast charging issues and found high temperature as the core problem. Similar comments were given by many other authors in their published works as well (Ajanovic, 2015; Husain, 2010; Chan et al., 2001; Westbrook and Westbrook, 2001; Ehsani et al., 2018; Rad et al., 2013; Erdinc et al., 2009).

A lot of studies have been on thermal management of lithium ion batteries (Wu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020a; Choudhari et al., 2020; Lyu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021b, 2020, 2021a; Heyhat et al., 2020; Chung and Kim, 2019; Ghaeminezhad et al., 2023). Despite all the hype of an EVs today, the critical issue of battery thermal management system in EVs has not​ been given much attention and hence demoralizes customer satisfaction on an EV. Hence, present study is intended to explore the thermal optimization of lithium-ion battery system for an EV. Firstly, design parameters for BTMS are discussed with the citation of research performed on thermal stability as a result of thermal runaway, and performance of an EV in subzero temperatures for BTMS. Moreover, the emphasis of this study is to review and analyze the different theoretical and practical heat transfer techniques applied by researchers on lithium-ion battery for better BTMS. Thus, the novel aspect of this paper leads to the discussion of the gap between researchers and manufacturers for battery thermal management in an EV that will give new direction to explode futuristic designs of battery for better BTMS in an EV.

Generally, an electric vehicle is the combination of an electric motor, a power electronics controller, the energy source in form of batteries and a mechanical transmission to drive the wheels. As the heart of the discussion is the energy source i.e., the lithium-ion batteries, so its overall configuration shall be considered. There are three key major components in a lithium-ion battery: a cathode, an anode and electrolyte made up of a lithium salt in a non-aqueous organic solvent. The electrolyte is required to have a high ionic conductivity and low viscosity with minimal volume; this is achieved by using mixture of solvents. In addition, a separator with a permeable membrane is placed in between the anode and cathode, preventing short circuit and allowing free movement of Li-ions. The charging phase uses the reverse direction and is referred to as intercalation (Liu et al., 2016). This movement of Li-ions and overall cell operation is shown in Fig. 1.

Some authors (Ciez and Whitacre, 2017; Rodriguez-Martinez et al., 2014) suggested that a prismatic pouch allows better thermal management due to its large surface area for heat dissipation and are cost effective too, but cylindrical batteries are still preferred by some because they are easily mass produced. These individual cells are wired together with voltage regulators, safety control devices and thermocouples to form modules and these modules are then packed with a BTMS and control devices. The value chain for electric car batteries comprises of seven steps (Dinger et al., 2017) as shown in Fig. 2.

Safety of any vehicle is the primary concern of the consumers. In an electric or a hybrid vehicle, safety is associated with the design of the battery pack. Li-ion batteries are considered as unstable and criticized for incendiary. Li-ion cells use volatile organic solvents like ethylene carbonate and ethyl methyl carbonate as the electrolyte. The standard accounts for heat generation from the RESS, specific RESS crash-test requirements and protection against short-circuit. Other safety standards relative to Li-ion batteries include IEC 62133, UL 2054, UL 2271 and IEEE 1625 or 1725 battery certifications (Ribière et al., 2012; Doughty et al., 2003). In spite of these tests, a number of changes in product designs make it impossible to test different aspects of a product safety in a single standard. Furthermore, products are designed to pass a particular standard, so an unexpected problem may pop up.

As the lithium-ion batteries take over other conventional power storage systems, an overview is needed to highlight how the international market has evolved. There are different metal-based batteries available in the market. Among them nickel-metal hydride (NiMH) and lead acid–based batteries have been in the market for a long time and are still used in many vehicles as primary or secondary source of power. These different electrochemical power sources are usually compared by their power and energy densities (Lemon and Miller, 2013). This comparison is important as it helps to determine the P/E ratio and adds reason to the dominance of Lithium-ion battery over other battery types. Fig. 3 also specifies the P/E regions which are crucial for EV, HEV and PHEVs. Here it is worth mentioning that the battery in an HEV stores a small amount of energy and is consequently small in size, since it recharges more often whilst driving. Therefore, it craves for more power than energy and so has a quite higher P/E value. In comparison, a PHEV is a medium P/E range vehicle as its performance requires battery to produce as much energy and power. In contrast to the hybrid vehicles, a purely electric vehicle requires more energy as compared to power and thus has a low P/E ratio.

An added benefit of lithium-ion batteries is that they do not possess a “memory effect” (Oswal et al., 2010). This phenomenon is a well-known drawback of NiMH batteries, gradually causing capacity loss as batteries are set to recharge before full depletion and thus battery life is reduced. In simple words NiMH batteries tend to drain when parked for long duration. As mentioned earlier that there is less energy requirement for HEV due to low P/E ratio, so a smaller, lighter, and equally efficient battery will lessen the weight and cut the cost of the pack entirely.

As per the production and usage of Electric Vehicles, China, and USA rank at top positions. Patil (2008) conducted a study on the developments of Li-ion battery in China. Report suggested that the Li-ion battery industry in China has grown at an annual average rate of more than 30% in the past decade and is still on rise. Moreover, some studies (Lowe et al., 2010) based on the value chain of batteries in USA also predicted in 2010 that the Lithium ion would replace most of the lead acid and NiMH based batteries by 2017. In addition, the Deutsche Bank (Ciez and Whitacre, 2017) assessed that with increasing production and shift of focus toward​ Li-ion technology, the cost of a battery would decline from $650/kWh in 2009 to $325/kWh by 2020.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of batteries for BTMS

ce:section.ce:para: Thermal runaway (TR) can be defined as a process initiated by production of more internal heat than what a battery can dissipate, often leading to a destructive result (Jiang and Zhang, 2015; William, 2015). This can happen as a result of a battery’s mechanical, electrochemical, or thermal failure. These three abuses that lead to thermal runaway are illustrated (William, 2015) in Fig. 4, whereas the details are as follows:

To explore the performance of Li-ion batteries under mechanical misuse, Zhang et al. (2015b) and Choi et al. (2013) coupled modeling techniques to envisage any mechanical deformation and failure in Lithium battery. Ali et al. (2013) and Lai et al. (2014) experimented and simulated and the deformation patterns and void compactions of Li-ion battery under constrained compression experiments. The results showed agreement with between finite element analysis and the experiments performed. A notable addition by Sahraei et al. (2012) and Sahraei et al. (2014) was the formulation of an enhanced constitutive model for Li-ion battery optimization and safety checks. Similarly, many researchers agreed that the mechanical abuses of lithium ion battery may cause thermal runaway if batteries are not designed for safety (Zhang and Wierzbicki, 2015; Xia et al., 2014a; Ren et al., 2014; Xia et al., 2014b). Thermal runaway caused by electrical failure is typically associated with increased current flow, power dissipation; short circuiting, overcharging, and overcharging can be of concern here too. Lin et al. (2013) and Ouyang et al. (2015a) used X-ray diffraction method and incremental capacity analysis respectively to analyze the impact of temperature difference initiated by the overcharge abuse between the cathode and the anode. Lamb et al. (2014) investigated the performance and abuse tolerance of cells at higher rates of overcharge. The findings from above experiments suggested overcharging as a leading cause of temperature elevation in the lithium-ion battery and the electrolyte oxidation caused battery to swell at higher temperatures. Zhang et al. (2015a) used electrochemical and physical characterization to investigate the capacity declining of over-discharged batteries during long-term cycle. As a result, deposition of copper on the anode hindered the lithium intercalation into/de-intercalation from the anode. Ren et al. (2017a) developed an electrochemical-thermal model to forecast the highly interacting electrochemical and thermal characteristics of Li-ion battery under overcharge. Guo et al. (2016), Erol et al. (2014) and Kim et al. (2014a) also reported similar results by analysis using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction and different measurement models. Thermal failure can be caused by loose cell packaging or increased resistance inside the battery. Wu et al. (2018) worked on the evaluation of thermal stability of lithium ion batteries with internal and external heating mode. It was observed that efficient heat dissipation may stop all internal exothermal reactions, and that thermal runaway of the cell is unavoidable when voltage drop is generated by cathode reaction and separator melting. After gathering and analyzing typical data periods, Zheng et al. (2013) determined that increment in internal or contact resistance caused thermal failure. Appropriate steps are followed to fix the situation, and the underlying safety concern is resolved. The recommended diagnostic procedures, as well as those provided by others, can be employed on-site for EV diagnosis without the need for laboratory analysis (Feng et al., 2016b; Ouyang et al., 2015b; Lamb and Orendorff, 2014; Jana et al., 2015)

To address the mechanisms that allow thermal runaway to damage lithium-ion battery, Yayathi et al. (2016) considered the net energy produced during thermal runaway as distributions between the cell body and hot gases. At various stages of charge, Boston Power Swing 5300, Samsung 18650-26F, and MoliCel 18650-J Li-ion battery packs were tested. It was discovered that state-of-charge and starting temperature had an inverse correlation. Similar tests were performed by Chris et al. (2004) and Doughty (2006) who explained that if heat dissipation is unavailable, the temperature of lithium ion cell increases swiftly. As the temperature exceeds the onset temperature (60 °C-100 °C), increased electrolyte reduction reaches the acceleration temperature (160 °C-170 °C). Additional heating triggers thermal runaway and temperatures range between 170 °C-200 °C. Coman et al. (2016) presented a mathematical model to evaluate the thermal behavior of a Li-ion battery in case of thermal runaway. The findings revealed that if isentropic flow equations are omitted, the thermal runaway would commence impulsively at spot where venting should occur. Thus the model reveals good agreement with the experimental works of other researchers (Feng et al., 2014, 2016a, 2015; Kalhoff et al., 2015) and therefore proves the importance of including venting. After the TR incident of Boeing 787 Dreamliner, the NASA Engineering and Safety Center (NESC) addressed the safety concerns associated with design of Li-ion batteries. NESC suggested manufacturers to ensure that design would prevent a catastrophic event caused by TR. In Addition, the manufacturers must demonstrate that propagation of TR to surrounding cells was prevented (William, 2015).

Another issue that battery thermal management has to cope is the performance of EVs and HEVs at extreme temperatures, which includes freezing cold temperatures and burning hot temperatures. Any BTMS must be able to handle the given thermal conditions and be as efficient as in optimum temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working of battery at sub-zero temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Sub-zero temperatures are a common climatic phase in North America especially Canada and also in most European countries including Russia. This condition demands the lithium ion battery to be designed cautiously as temperature fluctuation is of utmost importance and might become a source of setbacks. Low temperatures can significantly alter the chemical viability of Li- ion battery and decrease its power and energy potential by 40%. Factors like the overall cell design, its shape, and the suitability of the electrolytic solution in cold environments have been cited as the downfall factors. Another key obstacle is the inability to use regenerative braking at low temperatures, generally below 5 °C.

Martel et al. (2016) investigated the degradation of battery at subzero temperatures. The study examined the optimum process’ responsiveness and how it affected critical areas of PHEV operating parameter such battery state-of-charge control. Furthermore, battery core temperature regulation, as well as fuel costs for both driving load distribution and temperature regulation, were assessed using vehicle operating costs once the parameters were set. Jaguemont et al. (2016) performed experimentation by charging Li-ion battery at atmospheric conditions and discharged it under extremely cold conditions. The results of four distinct temperatures were analyzed using the influence of seasonal fluctuations in temperature. Tripathy et al. (2016) investigated the energy and power decline at subzero temperatures and determined the minimum temperatures for Li-ion battery to meet the driving cycle power requirements. Additionally, the effects of regenerative braking and self-heating on battery performance are discussed. Vega et al. (2015) used an approximate battery model to illustrate how low temperatures affect the battery’s parameters. The goals were to determine the battery’s internal resistance and heat capacity by monitoring the open circuit voltage over time and gaining knowledge about how to manage the battery’s power. Similarly, many other studies (Alihosseini and Shafaee, 2021; Rahn and Wang, 2013; Godina et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2015; Ji et al., 2013; Aris and Shabani, 2017) highlighted the effect of subzero temperatures on Li-ion battery performance.

To overcome technical barriers for penetration in market it is imperative to analyze how the Li-ion battery operates in hot climatic conditions, more specifically its discharge characteristics are studied. Considering this question, Hosseinzadeh et al. (2018) validated the performance of the battery model for a temperature span of 5 °C to 45 °C. Results highlighted that the Li-ion battery performance was better at 45 °C and energy efficiency and charge increased at this temperature. However, Mamyrbayeva et al. (2013) presented his analysis on performance of Li-ion battery and evaluation of its efficiency at different operational temperatures. Findings revealed that the battery showed surge in capacity fade of pouch type battery with increase of temperature. The discharge capacity at 25 °C was reportedly greater than that at 0 °C and 45 °C, because of secondary active material loss dominating the other losses. Leng et al. (2015) explored the influence of high temperature on cyclic aging rate of Li-ion battery, using electrochemistry-based model in temperature range of 25 °C to 55 °C. The maximum charge storage reportedly decreased at elevated temperature due to the alteration of the surface coatings on the electrodes and structural/phase changes of the lithium electrode. Raut et al. (2017) investigated approaches to raise the operating temperature of Li-ion batteries. Thermal stability and the safe functioning of Li-ion batteries were regarded as critical performance indicators in hybrid cars and other high thermal applications. The results suggested that lithium ion cells with ion gel membranes were operable at 100 °C without breakdown. A strong correlation between battery temperature and discharge activities was identified by Hasan et al. (2017) and Sayed et al. (2017), which resulted in an excessive degradation of the battery. Chen and Chen and Li (2014), and other authors (Basu et al., 2016; Goutam et al., 2015; Kabir and Demirocak, 2017) also developed experimental and theoretical models to emphasize the effect of high atmospheric temperature on performance of Li-ion battery. Conclusively, the case established by the above researchers establishes that lithium ion battery operates to the best of its ability and with maximum efficiency in optimum climatic regions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Models for battery thermal management strategies
ce:section.ce:para: To provide an effective BTMS, scientists and researchers have used many different approaches. Following sections discuss the numerical/theoretical and experimental approaches used in some of the latest works:

The thermal modeling of a Li-ion battery is carried out using mathematical expressions and descriptions based on the specific thermal conditions, thus helping to predict the performance of the system. These expressions are developed using the established energy, electrochemical, heat generation and transfer equations with the boundary constraints of the working environment. Methods like computational fluid dynamics (CFD) are essential to assess the thermal characteristics of a Li-ion battery. This section reviews the prominent theoretical studies on BTMS by categorizing them into battery temperature distribution models, Thermal runaway models and numerical studies of BTMS. The summary of these studies is shown in Table 1 and the heat generation equations used by various authors are listed in Table 2.

Jeon (2014) projected the temperature dispersal within the cylindrical 18,650 battery cells using a transient thermo-electric model. The results indicated that the rise in temperature during the discharge cycle was greater than that during the charging cycle of the battery, but the difference decreased as C-rates increased. Thermal behavior was found to be relative to entropy change and applied current. Dong et al. (2018) employed one combination of an electrochemical-thermal linked module to explore the thermal characteristics of the charging/discharging cycles of a Li-ion battery. A detailed analysis indicated a very large current flow led to termination of discharge/charge operation and the battery was more susceptible to overheat leading to thermal runaway during discharge. Moreover, cut-off voltage at high charging rates also caused rapid heat generation initiating thermal runaway of Li-ion batteries. A 2D thermal model of pouch shaped cell was developed using CFD by Samba et al. (2014). The purpose was to forecast the temperature dispersal over the surface of battery, using the heat generation and thermal properties at different environmental conditions. Baba et al. (2014) presented model for understanding the localized heat generation across the electrode plane due to electrochemical reaction. The electrochemical-thermal model was integrated with the quasi-3D porous electrode solver. Temperature estimations generated using the ESP model the inside of electrodes and cells were found to be in good agreement with real experimental data. Damay et al. (2015) proposed a lumped model using quasi-steady state assumption, having physical and geometrical properties. The heat generation model and electrical model are used to provide the heat source modeling and the electrical in an equivalent electric circuit. These coupled thermal and heat generation models help to estimate temperature variation. Wang et al. (2015a) employed a simplified thermal resistance power circuit model that was strongly linked to surface temperature variation and heat flow density. The steady state temperature for the same chip with two distinct shapes was determined using the thermal profile model. Wang et al. (2017) forecasted optimal thermal management of Li-ion batteries using a unified artificial neural network-based model. This entails the incorporation of factors such as health status and current into battery models. Richter et al. (2017) investigated the aging of Li-ion batteries at various temperatures and charging cycles. The findings showed that capacity for storage lowered at elevated temperature, and the core temperature surged by a factor of about 2.5 during aging from a pure state of health at 100% capacity to 58% capacity.

Different thermal runaway scenarios of Li-ion battery are also modeled using CFD. Kim et al. (2014b) simulated the consequences of temperature variation and thermal runaway on the simple operation of a battery cell. These simulations assisted in the improvement of temperature regulation techniques and can be used in the design of BTMS; with improved battery performance by up to 58.4%. Chiu et al. (2014) demonstrated the incidental behavior of Li-ion battery in nail penetration testing by an electrochemical model. The author used electrochemical governing equations and thermal abuse equations to consider mass and charge transfer effect and predict temperature variation accurately. Accordingly, both the inception of battery thermal degradation and temperature profile of cell were obtained. Lee et al. (2015) explored the delay in thermal degradation of battery using an electrochemical thermal model of Li-ion battery using resistive heating. Simulations revealed that the probability of thermal-runaway occurring at higher power load level was more than that at lower levels. Oh and Epureanu (Oh and Epureanu, 2016; Oh et al., 2014) explored the differences between thermal swelling and swelling caused by electrochemical abuse. The influence of thermal conduction and thermal expansion coefficient on temperature fluctuations of battery cells and packs was explored using a coupled 3-D finite thermal–structural model. The simulation and results from thermal augmentation at various pulse excitations are presented in Fig. 5.

Ren et al. (2017b) coupled electrochemical-thermal model with overcharge-to-thermal-runaway model for estimating Li-ion battery thermal attributes under overcharge situations. The electrolyte oxidation reaction and the reaction between accumulated lithium and electrolyte were discovered to generate excessive heat during the overcharge operation. The results suggested that boosting the electrolyte’s oxidation capacity and enhancing the thermal runaway’s start temperature are two efficient strategies to improve the overcharge operation of Li-ion batteries. Ping et al. (2017) investigated various cooling and atmospheric conditions to get overview of the various process parameter necessary for the development of BTMS. When the temperature was minimal, the airflow velocity offered efficient protection against overheating, but it was less efficient when the temperature was higher. The thickness of the airflow stream was also important for lithium battery tolerance at high temperatures.

Panchal et al. (2018) employed a neural network technique to develop a thermal model, examining the temperature variations of primary surface of battery under several discharge/charge conditions. This study shows increased discharge rates cause an increase in surface temperature distributions of the battery. This neural network was also used to envisage the thermal performance of Li-ion battery for air cooling (Panchal et al., 2016a), mini-channel cold plates (Panchal et al., 2016b) and water cooling (Panchal et al., 2017). According to the findings, enhancing discharge rates increased the heat fluxes. According to Saw et al. (2017), an uneven cooling would cause a large difference in temperature variation and impair the battery pack’s life expectancy. Varying aluminum foams used as thermal conductivity enhancers were modeled in correlation of Nusselt number and other numerical properties using CFD simulation. The results show that low PPI and high porosity of Al foam offers highest thermal performance and lowest flow resistances. He et al. (2014) used CFD modeling to capture the detailed fluid dynamics in a convection based BTMS, measuring change in values of temperature and pressure. The results as shown in Fig. 8 agreed with experimental results and valuable to optimize the design of battery module. Greco et al. (2014) used thermal circuit technique combined with thermal model of the heat pipe to compare 1D, 3D, and analytical model of a prismatic Li-ion battery cell. As a result, the 1D strategy was able to forecast that heat pipes may effectively regulate the temperature of a battery pack with limited surface areas. To cater the temperature imbalance caused by varying charge/discharge rates, Bahiraei et al. (2017b) modeled battery temperature distribution for a simplified electrochemical model to solve it in 1D to assess its streamlined behavior as well as the Ohmic heat generation is evaluated in 3D. This proposed model helped to minimize the estimated time for battery modeling which allowed simulation of the active or passive BTMS in minimum time. In another study (Bahiraei et al., 2017a) a dual channel cooling system gave lesser maximum temperature and constant temperature variation than a single channel cooling system. One such study by Zhao et al. (2018) involving channeled liquid flow revealed that increasing the coolant flow rate significantly lowered the temperature gain caused by increasing the discharge/charge C-rates. Moreover, augmenting the heat exchange area improved the temperature uniformity, but did not lower the module temperature. In contrast, increase in the interfacing area of the battery had adverse effects. Similar, comparisons were made by De Vita et al. (2017) discussing an air and liquid based cooling techniques.

Lu et al. (2018) investigated the influence of cooling channel size and air supply on the thermal performance of battery packs using a staggered battery pack layout. Maximum temperature, the space utilization and the energy efficiency factors exhibited significant influence on the battery cooling. According to findings, the optimum cooling performance was attained when the airflow intake and outlet were positioned on the top of the battery pack, and that cooling energy efficiency declined significantly as the number of batteries increased horizontally. Mohammadian et al. (2017, 2015) and Mohammadian et al. (2016) evaluated the combined impact of using a pin fin heat sink and porous metal foam as BTMS. The effects of porous foam, several types of pin fins, air flow inlet temperature and velocity on uniform and highest temperature inside the battery pack were comprehensively explored. According to the findings, using a pin-fin heat sink was only appropriate for low air flow velocities, and porous aluminum pin fins were ineffective. Similar approach was used by Wang and Li (2017), Tong et al. (2016) and that by Choi and Kang (2014). Moreover, Xie et al. (2017b) and Yu et al. (2014) studied the effects of three parameters, the air inlet and outlet angle, and the width of the air flow channel between battery cells for forced air cooling system.

Hong et al. (2018) enhanced the cooling capacity of the parallel air-cooled BTMS using secondary vent. The effects of the secondary vent’s size and location on the BTMS’ cooling operation were explored. The findings revealed that the position of the secondary vent had significant impact on the battery pack’s highest temperature difference, and that the cooling operation improved as the size of the secondary vent increased (Chen et al., 2017). The schematic setup for this study is shown in Fig. 6.

Wang et al. (2014) also performed similar analysis with agreement to these results. Jiang et al. (2017) explored the role of baffles on enhancing air fluid heat transfer with a tube-shell battery module using forced air-cooling system. The thermal properties of the tube-shell battery pack were examined, and the findings suggest that the baffles alter the course of the air fluid flow, enhancing fluid contact and heat transfer performance. Another aspect studied for convective BTM is the arrangement of the lithium ion cells. Yang et al. (2015) investigated the impact of longitudinal and transverse space on battery cooling performance using aligned and staggered arrays. For staggered arrays, the highest temperature increase was proportional to the longitudinal interval under a given cooling air flow rate, whereas for aligned arrays, it was contrary. For both aligned and staggered setups, increasing the transverse interval causes the battery temperature to rise. as shown in Fig. 7.

Zhang et al. (2014) proposed a novel active battery BTMS to explore the effects of fluctuations in atmospheric temperature, humidity, and thermal load for the direct cabin air blow technique. The results reveal that the approach was more efficient in achieving high exergy than the refrigerant circulation method. Erb et al. (2017) investigated the effects of cell size on air-cooling costs in aligned battery packs. According to the findings, a cell size that minimizes cooling cost may be identified for a given pack requirements.

The proposed cooling technique may be applied to successfully forecast the minimal cost cell size for cylindrical cells in crossflow as long as the required assumptions are met but still the performance of battery using air cooling is quite and new techniques should be used for better BTMS.

A lot studies have been done using PCMs (Jilte et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2020b; Wu et al., 2022; Safdari et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2021) to control the temperature of battery for battery thermal management. Luo et al. (2022) discussed the usage of phase change material (PCM) based passive cooling and heating for BTMS. The authors reviewed several BTMS strategies such as PCM, phase change fluids, flexible PCMs, hybrid cooling, air and liquid cooling, and heat pipe-based cooling. Organic PCM flammability and inorganic PCM stability were critical for improved BTMS. Various challenges with the use of PCMs in BTMS were also highlighted.

Lin et al. (2021) studied several thermal management models that forecast heat generation, heat transmission, and temperature dispersal inside the battery cell, module, and pack. The advantages and disadvantages of different BTMS, as well as the heat generation and modeling​ methodologies of li-ion batteries, were explored. The recent growing BTMS techniques were also explored, and a new BTMS framework based on cyber hierarchy and interactional network was developed.

Yue et al. (2021) investigated a hybrid cooling approach for Li-ion battery pack under dynamic conditions. The study combined micro heat pipes, forced air convection and intermittent water spray as depicted in Fig. 8. According to findings, the hybrid cooling system resulted in maximum temperature drop of 29.6 °C and temperature fluctuation to 1.6 °C which were 21% and 57% lower than thermal control system without water spray function.

Zhang et al. (2021) designed and explored numerically the hybrid BTMS using PCM, liquid cooling and heat pipe (Fig. 9). Based on the Adaptive-Kriging High dimensional model representation approach, a numerical surrogate model was developed. According to the global sensitivity analysis, the thermal conductivity of the PCM layer, the thickness of the PCM layer, the length of the heat pipe, and the inlet velocity of water all had a significant impact on the maximum temperature and temperature dispersal of the battery pack. The numerical model evaluated for cycling and thermal runaway performed best in terms of heat dissipation, with a maximum temperature drop of 30 °C and insignificant temperature variations.

Zhuang et al. (2021) investigated the novel BTMS by optimizing the battery pack configuration and employed intelligent cooling technique by adjusting air flow to mitigate the increasing battery temperature. According to results, the proposed BTMS could save the energy 76.4% compared to pristine case and lowered the temperature variation from 1.5 °C to 0.6 °C.

Jilte et al. (2021) numerically investigated the BTMS employing two layers of nanoparticles enriched PCM. The study evaluated the cooling efficacy of BTMS at ambient temperatures ranging from 30 °C to 40 °C. The results revealed that even at a high ambient temperature of 40 °C, the BTMS configuration based on nanoparticles enriched PCM kept the cell temperature below 46 °C. Hekmat and Molaeimanesh (2020) integrated two hybrid cooling techniques of PCM and cooling water pipes for Li-ion battery packs. For thermal characterization, the study investigated five cooling scenarios, two active, one passive, and two conventional. The findings demonstrated the effectiveness of PCM-based passive cooling, however the time required to cool down the battery pack to 30 °C was greater than in the hybrid cases. Furthermore, the hybrid cases demonstrated maximum temperature reduction and enhanced thermal performance.

Wang et al. (2021c) numerically evaluated the PCM based passive BTMS of Li-ion battery. The influence of design factors such as thermal conductivity, viscosity, latent heat, and PCM thickness, as well as the thermal conductivity of the unit shell, was also examined. According to the findings, PCM units could effectively reduce the maximum temperature and maximum temperature variation inside the battery cells at the end of discharging, leading to better heat storage capability.

Ling et al. (2021) employed inorganic PCM for BTMS and utilized a multiscale encapsulation approach to address the inherent issues with inorganic PCM sodium acetate trihydrate (SAT)-Urea. This method involved encapsulating expanded graphite to increase thermal conductivity and eliminate leakage. The inorganic PCM regulated the maximum temperature below 52.3 °C and the maximum temperature variation below 4 °C in a 20-cell battery pack, according to the results.

Cao et al. (2022) investigated the passive cooling of Li-ion battery packs using an inorganic composite PCM sodium acetate trihydrate and expanded graphite as shown in Fig. 10. The experimental results were used to validate the numerical model. The heat storage of composite PCM was split into two phases: latent heat storage and thermochemical heat storage. According to the findings, the thermal overrun of the battery was avoided by including composite PCM due to its significant thermal storage capacity as compared to the case without composite PCM.

Wang et al. (2022) packed hydrated salt into paraffin PCM before adsorbing the hybrid PCM onto expanded graphite porous material. The resulting composite PCM demonstrated excellent form stability, high thermal conductivity, and heat storage capacity. Fire resistance attribute was provided by the use of hydrated salt and expanded graphite. As proven, the prepared composite PCM exhibited efficacy in preventing Li-ion battery overheat, indicating tremendous promise in Li-ion battery thermal management in terms of affordability, safety, and cooling performance.

Lazrak et al. (2018) attempted to make the temperature distribution inside a Li-ion battery pack and its cells as uniform as possible using PCM. After the phase transition within the PCM was completed, numerical and experimental findings demonstrated that the new system was able to lower the system temperature. Malik et al. (2017) investigated a composite PCM of various thicknesses to develop a pack level passive thermal management system. The battery surface temperature drops from 56.5 °C with no cooling to 36.5 °C at a high discharge rate of 4C when 6 mm thick phase change composite material plates are used. Furthermore, when compared to no cooling and liquid cooling, the temperature gradient with composite PCM was substantially lower. Ling et al. (2015) demonstrated that heat buildup in PCMs causes thermal management system failures. To minimize heat generation and keep the temperature peak value under 50 °C in all cycles, a hybrid system combining PCMs and forced air convection was adopted. Similar results were reported by Fathabadi (2014).

Xie et al. (2017a) explored a composite PCM based BTMS with air cooling. For charge/discharge cycles, the air flow rate and PCM liquid fraction were evaluated., graphical results are shown in Fig. 11. The integrated system’s cooling impact was determined to be superior to that of the air-cooling system. Variations in air flow rate and ambient temperature, on the other hand, had no effect on heat dissipation of phase transition cooling.

Wilke et al. (2017) studied nail penetration on Li-ion pack with and without composite PCM, to examine its effectiveness for avoiding thermal runaway spread in a single cell, as illustrated in Fig. 12. Tests revealed that when short-circuit occurred; the packs without PCM propagated it completely while those equipped with PCM show no propagation. Additionally, the application of PCM reduces the maximum temperature caused in nearby cells by at least 60 °C. In brief, composite PCM has been discovered to give passive protection at a low weight and cost while lowering design complexity.

Zou et al. (2018) examined the performance of BTMS utilizing various carbon nanotube and MWCNT/graphene based composite PCM combinations. The optimum additive quantities for certain combinations were investigated to increase thermal conductivity while also limiting a quick temperature increase in liquid PCM. When compared to other combinations, composite PCM demonstrated the best heat transfer enhancement, with the better temperature increase/decrement and a significant increase in thermal conductivity. Samimi et al. (2016) investigated the influence of carbon fiber packing within the PCM’s thermal performance. The results demonstrate that PCM composites with higher fraction of carbon fiber offered uniform temperature distribution and better effective thermal conductivity. Several authors (Malik et al., 2016; Greco and Jiang, 2016) observed that employing graphite can augment the thermal conductivity of PCMs by up to 70
W m
− 1
K
− 1
.

Al-Zareer et al. (2018, 2017) presented a BTMS that used liquid propane as PCM to eliminate excess heat from Li-ion batteries, and propane vapor to cool the components of the battery not covered in liquid propane; this arrangement is shown in Fig. 13. The findings reveal that propane offers effective temperature regulation of the batteries during high and constant charge/discharge cycles. Zhao et al. (2017) demonstrated the efficiency of a PCM-based BTMS in cooling by switching the hollow mandrel in a cylindrical battery with a PCM-filled mandrel. Variables such as PCM material, PCM core size, and distribution were utilized to assessing the thermal performance, PCM solidification time, and pack compactness for design optimization. When thicker PCM cores were inserted in the inside batteries, a consistent temperature distribution was achieved. Furthermore, the internal cooling system was discovered as space-saving design that aids in the battery pack’s high energy density.

Although PCM are based BTMS are proved to be efficient but still the efficiency of battery is low due to low value of thermal conductivity of PCM, and various different combinations must be used for better BTMS. Therefore, heat pipe based PCM based cooling proved to be efficient for BTMS.

Lately the heat pipe BTMSs have received substantial research focus since they are an excellent approach for conserving energy in electric cars. This concept makes use of the bidirectional nature of heat pipes, which allows the evaporator and condenser of the heat pipe to swap in response to real-time cooling/heating requirements. So, heat pipes (Chen et al., 2021; He et al., 2022; Jiang and Qu, 2019; Alihosseini and Shafaee, 2021) are broadly used for battery thermal management.

Liang et al. (2018) evaluated the influence of factors like coolant flow rate, surrounding temperature, coolant temperature, and start-up time on the thermal efficacy of heat pipe based BTMS (HP-BTMS). According to findings, the thermal performance of the HP-BTMS improves marginally while atmospheric temperature declined but remains unchanged as coolant temperature decreases. The heat transfer flow between the heat pipe and the coolant is depicted in Fig. 14. Ye et al. (2016) employed heat pipe to improve cooling capacity and system temperature uniformity. HP thermal control system was sensitive to coolant temperature fluctuation, and the drop in maximum heat pipe evaporator temperature was almost equal to the decrease in coolant temperature. The cooling technique had significant impact on battery thermal response and has been identified as a viable method for rapid charging lithium ion battery packs. It can also be verified from the work of Yuan et al. (2016).

Rao et al. (2013) investigated the use of heat pipe to reduce the overall cost of electric vehicles and enhance the cycle time of Li-ion batteries. The maximum temperature could be regulated below 50 °C while the heat generation rate was less than 50 W, according to the analysis. Authors (Rao et al., 2014) explored an oscillating heat pipe (OHP) based BTMS. It was determined that the higher temperature component of the battery might be located near the OHP condensation region. Furthermore, OHP should not be placed horizontally in order to reduce the airflow resistance of the working fluid contained by OHP. Zou et al. (2016) paired a heat pipe battery cooling/preheating system with a heat pump air conditioning system to evaluate their performance under a variety of operating situations. The analysis reveals that a system intended to fulfill the fundamental cooling requirement with an extra battery chiller is a cost-effective solution for thermal control of battery pack, adding 20% more cooling capacity without increasing input power. As a result, the heat pipe thermal performance in preheating mode outperforms cooling generated by gravity. Hong et al. (2015) studied the heat transmission potential of the ultra-thin looping heat pipe, they found similar outcomes.

To manage aberrant heat emissions in Lithium ion batteries, Yamada et al. (2017) designed a hybrid cooling system using paraffin wax as PCM and heat pipes. As a result, the scientists were able to establish that using the suggested cooling system enhanced the time required to attain temperatures resulting to thermal runaway in the modeled battery pack from 104 s to 708 s, compared to 104 s when using no cooling device. Ye et al. (2015) optimized the heat pipe under steady-state and transient situations and discovered that placing a cylinder vortex generator in front of the coolant stream of the heat pipe condensers improved temperature uniformity. The investigations suggest that the heat pipe based TMS improves battery heat control during rapid charging. When combined with air-cooled heat pipe TMS, the system becomes infeasible for fast charging at the battery pack level due to poor specific heat capacity.

As most cooling methods target outer surface of battery cells, a high thermal resistance increases the in-cell temperature gradients, accelerating aging and performance reduction. So, Worwood et al. (2017) developed an internal heat pipe system using metallic disks as BTMS. Highly aggressive electrical loading conditions were studied and indicated this strategy when compared to single tab cooling reduced the cell thermal resistance by 68% and also predicted similar performance of relatively complex pack level double tab cooling system. The viability of employing sintered copper–water heat pipes for various conditions was assessed by Wang et al. (2015b). The findings demonstrate that the suggested approach could keep the battery surface temperature under 40 °C if the battery generates under 10 W/cell, while lowering the temperature to 70 °C under extraordinary thermal stress conditions (20–40 W/cell). Air cooling of the cell surface might not efficiently cool the core of the cells due to the limited thermal conductivity of Li-ion cells. Shah et al. (2016) investigate the cooling of Li-ion cells through an annular channel running across the cell’s axis. Heat pipe studies discovered a drop in temperature of 18 °C to 20 °C in the cell core, depending on heat pipe size. Surprisingly, the findings were same when a thin metal rod was utilized. In summary using heat pipe successfully prevents overheating caused by malfunction and or short circuiting, as depicted in graphical form in Fig. 15.

Liquid cooling is considered a effective approach to maintain the battery temperature in a suitable range. It allows significant improvement in its efficiency and has been popular among many researchers, who keep experimenting different liquid-based substances to enhance BTMS.

Zhen et al. (2016) investigated a liquid cooling system for heat conduction in lithium-ion battery packs based on a mini-channel cold-plate. The influences of channel number, intake flow rates, flow direction, and channel width were investigated. The system was shown to have outstanding cooling efficiency, and the temperature could be significantly decreased by increasing the mass flow rate at the intake. Additionally, the temperature uniformity was significantly improved. Considering constant current discharge rates, such case of liquid cooling was studied by Malik et al. (2018). The results imply that the battery pack temperature could be kept within the acceptable range, with the battery pack’s discharge rate improving as the coolant temperature increases. Suman et al. (2016) evaluated the effect of operational variables such as coolant flow rate and discharge current on pack temperature, as illustrated in Fig. 16. Contact resistance was shown to have the greatest influence on the thermal efficiency of the pack.

Mondal et al. (2017) suggested using nanofluids for active thermal management, which are colloidal suspensions of nanoparticles in a base fluid. For two distinct flow arrangement, various nanofluids, and their influence on temperature distribution within standard battery packs were explored. According to findings, power efficiency, heat removal capabilities at high discharge rates, environmental conditions, and effective thermal safety were crucial to electric vehicles. An et al. (2017) proposed a flow boiling system in a mini channel using dielectric liquid, with experiment permitted at various discharge rates and flow Re numbers. It was revealed that a reasonably uniform temperature and voltage distribution given within the module at a greater discharge rate makes use of the advantage of boiling heat transfer with uniform temperature variation on cold plate. Furthermore, when the Re number of fluid increased, the voltage declined owing to the drop in temperature. Yang et al. (2016) presented liquid metal for thermal control by comparing the cooling capacity, pump power usage, and module temperature stability of the liquid metal cooling to that of water cooling. The results show that under comparable flow circumstances, a reduced and more consistent module temperature could be achieved, and the liquid metal cooling system requires less pump power usage. Furthermore, liquid metal has a strong cooling capability when coping with demanding situations such as high-power consumption, cell faults, and high ambient temperatures, according to reports.

Rao et al. (2017) investigated the application of liquid cooling with varying contact surface to a lithium-ion battery. The impact of length and intake velocities on thermal efficiency of an aluminum block used to transfer heat from battery to cooling water were examined. According to results, the system with changing contact surface outperforms the system with constant contact surface in terms of temperature uniformity. Xu et al. (2017) evaluated the effectiveness of a small channel cooling system at the battery module level for thermal runaway control for inside short circuits caused by nail penetration. Flow rate, heat stress response, nail penetration depth, and nail diameter are all taken into consideration. To summarize, mini-channel cooling at the cell level did not prevent battery degradation owing to thermal runaway propagation between battery cells. In addition, many authors (Karimi and Dehghan, 2014; He et al., 2017; Wei and Agelin-Chaab, 2018; Hosseinzadeh et al., 2017) have considered liquid cooling as efficient for high power usage, an environment friendly option and also used it in hybrid arrangement with other thermal management techniques. Although liquid based cooling for BTMS is proved to be efficient but still the performance of battery is low and more new liquids should be explored with high performance, and various different combinations must be used for better BTMS. Different thermal management systems with important constraints are given in Table 3.

This paper reviewed the practical and theoretical studies performed on battery thermal management systems and compared the annotations with optimization strategies used by real-time EV battery manufacturers. Some of the main concluded remarks as:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The study began by reviewing the design considerations for BTMS of a lithium ion battery from an HEV and EV manufacturer’s point of view, with understanding from different international standards. The statistics of international market and affordability of the system were also evaluated.

The second section covered the major concerns related to thermal sensitivity including thermal runaway and extreme weather conditions. The literature was reviewed to classify thermal runaway into mechanical failure, electrochemical failure and thermal failures of a battery. The extreme weather conditions looked upon the performance of EVs in sub-zero temperatures and hot climate regions. Being the core of this study, the performance of different BTMS like forced convection, PCM liquid/refrigerant-based cooling and heat pipes were reviewed. More combinations of the techniques should by different authors in their study.

Furthermore, various BTMS were analyzed for different constraints like temperature uniformity, charging/discharging rates and flow rates. The last part of this study focused on the real-time or prevalent BTMS developed by different EV battery manufacturers. In summary, air cooled systems are found to be widely used in hybrid vehicles whilst liquid cooling conquered the EV market. More techniques should be analyzed for young researchers in their future work.

Moreover, heat pipe cooling system needs more research to loosen its complexity and lure technologists. Thus, this review helped to generate a critical analysis of predicted thermal management solutions by researchers and those developed by manufactures in actual practice for better BTMS.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declares that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
